content.text: Two of the most practical features of destructuring are defaults — which let you supply fallback values for missing or absent properties — and aliasing, which lets you rename extracted values to any variable name you choose. Understanding both deeply is essential for writing clean, resilient React components that handle real-world data gracefully.
A default value in destructuring is a fallback that JavaScript uses when the extracted value would otherwise be
after the variable name in the destructuring pattern:
only when the extracted value is exactly
— is considered a real, intentional value and the default is skipped:
This distinction is significant in React. An API that returns
for a missing field is explicitly saying "no value" — and
will reach your component unchanged. If you need to handle both
, use a different strategy such as the nullish coalescing operator after extraction:

const { avatar } = apiResponse;
const finalAvatar = avatar ?? '/default-avatar.png'; // handles both null and undefined

A default value can be any JavaScript expression — a literal, a function call, a ternary, or even a reference to another variable. The expression is only evaluated if the default is actually needed (lazy evaluation):
This lazy evaluation is useful in React for avoiding expensive computations when optional props are provided, or for supplying dynamically generated fallback keys or IDs.

Aliasing — also called renaming — lets you extract a property into a local variable with a different name than the property key. The syntax in object destructuring is a colon after the property key, followed by the new variable name:
There are three common reasons to alias in destructuring:
When the same property name appears at multiple levels of a nested structure, or when a property name is already taken by another variable in scope, aliasing prevents conflicts:
can be aliased to something descriptive for the specific context where it is used:
3. Working with reserved words or invalid identifiers.
If a property key is a JavaScript reserved word or contains characters that cannot appear in an identifier, aliasing lets you extract it into a valid variable name:
Aliasing and defaults can be used together in a single destructuring entry. The syntax places the alias first (after the colon), then the default (after the equals sign):
Reading the pattern left to right:
is the property key to look up on the object;
is the fallback if the property is absent.
Defaults and aliasing shine brightest in React functional component parameters. Because React passes a single
object, the parameter destructuring is where you simultaneously define which props the component expects, what sensible defaults are, and — when needed — what local names they should carry:

This single parameter declaration acts as both a contract (documenting every accepted prop) and an implementation (supplying sensible defaults and clean local names), all without a single line of conditional logic inside the function body.

Aliasing is particularly useful when a parent component passes a generic prop name that needs a more specific name inside the child, or when wrapping a third-party component whose prop name conflicts with the parent's interface:

Defaults are especially important when applying nested destructuring, because an absent intermediate property causes a TypeError without a guard. The default is placed on the intermediate key, not on the inner properties:
You can also alias the nested container while guarding it:
Aliasing hides the original property name
Use defaults to document optional props.

In React components, a destructuring parameter with defaults serves as living documentation of which props are required (no default) and which are optional (has a default). This is more reliable than JSDoc comments, which can fall out of sync.
Rename a property when the original name is too generic (
), causes a collision (two levels both have
), or does not fit the component's local vocabulary (
Avoid aliasing just to shorten names.
saves a few characters but makes the code less searchable and less clear to future readers.
A default that is a simple literal (
) is easy to understand at a glance. Defaults that involve complex expressions or function calls can make the parameter signature hard to read.
Be careful with mutable default values.

creates a new array or object each time the default is used (because the expression is evaluated fresh for each call). This is safe in destructuring — unlike some languages where a mutable default is shared across all uses.

— the colon separates the lookup key from the local variable name.

— evaluated only when the property is actually absent, not on every call.
removes the original property name from scope — only the alias is available as a variable.
, defaults in the parameter destructuring replace the need for
checks inside the function body, making the component's contract clear and concise.
when you need to handle both
— destructuring defaults alone cannot do this.

Mastering defaults and aliasing turns destructuring from a shorthand for property access into a full-featured tool for defining component interfaces, handling optional data, and writing self-documenting code that does not require runtime conditional logic to be safe and correct.
